description: The provided image displays a publisher-level Check for updates notification badge rather than a scientific research figure containing experimental data. Visually, the image comprises a gray square background featuring a circular emblem with a red bookmark-shaped central icon surrounded by a yellow ring, accompanied by the black text Check for updates printed immediately below the emblem. No material characterization data—such as scanning or transmission electron micrographs, X-ray diffraction patterns, mechanical stress–strain curves, or optical micrographs relevant to the Fe–30Mn–10Al–1.5C–xBe low-density steel system—are observable. The figure caption is empty, and no citation context is supplied. Consequently, no information regarding sample identifiers, heat treatment conditions, phase assemblages, grain morphologies, defect structures, or quantitative property metrics can be extracted or verified from this visual content. The image functions solely as a publishing-platform user-interface element and does not provide evidence for the paper's stated focus on microstructure and mechanical properties.

This figure presents X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of as-cast Fe–30Mn–10Al–1.5C–xBe (x = 0, 0.5, 1.0, 1.5) low-density steels. Four stacked diffractograms correspond to beryllium contents of 0Be, 0.5Be, 1.0Be, and 1.5Be. The 0Be alloy exhibits peaks exclusively indexed to the γ-austenite phase (e.g., (111), (200), (220), (311)). As Be content increases, additional phases emerge: the 0.5Be pattern remains predominantly γ-austenite with minor indications of compound formation; the 1.0Be spectrum reveals diffraction peaks assigned to Be11Fe and Be2Fe intermetallic compounds together with δ-ferrite; the 1.5Be pattern displays the most complex phase assemblage, containing γ-austenite, δ-ferrite, Be11Fe, Be12Fe, Be2Fe, and BeC, with labeled reflections such as (110) δ, (301) and (112) Be11Fe, and (413) Be12Fe. The progressive appearance of δ-ferrite and multiple Be-rich intermetallics with increasing Be addition indicates that beryllium destabilizes the single-phase austenite matrix and promotes the formation of secondary phases in the as-cast microstructure, consistent with subsequent microscopic observations cited in the text.

This figure presents optical micrographs (OM) of as-cast Fe–30Mn–10Al–1.5C–xBe low-density steel strips with varying beryllium contents. Panel (a) shows the 0Be alloy exhibiting a fully austenitic (γ) microstructure with non-uniform cast morphology. Panel (b) for 0.5Be reveals the initial precipitation of black point-like beryllium compounds attached to the austenite matrix. Panels (c) and (d) correspond to 1.0Be and 1.5Be, respectively, where prominent Chinese character-shaped Be–Fe phase compounds appear at austenite/ferrite interfaces alongside substantial δ-ferrite formation within the γ matrix. With increasing Be content, the volume fraction of these intermetallic compounds increases, encroaching on the austenite matrix; concurrently, the ferrite content decreases when comparing 1.5Be to 1.0Be, accompanied by visibly finer ferrite grains in the 1.5Be alloy. The scale bar in all images is 50 μm. These observations demonstrate that Be addition fundamentally alters the as-cast microstructure by precipitating Be–Fe intermetallic phases and promoting ferrite formation, though excessive Be reduces ferrite volume fraction by occupying austenite sites.

Figure 3 presents bright-field TEM micrographs and selected area diffraction patterns of as-cast Fe–30Mn–10Al–1.5C–0.5Be low-density steel. Panel (a) shows overall precipitate morphology with a 5 μm scale bar, where red box A and green box B mark regions for detailed analysis. Panels (b) and (c) provide enlarged 1 μm-scale views of areas A and B, respectively, each with a circle indicating the SADP acquisition site on bright-contrast precipitates. Panels (d) and (e) display the corresponding SADPs, both indexed as the Be12Fe intermetallic phase along the [-113] and [001] zone axes, with labeled diffraction spots including 251, 152, 301 and 101, 202. The figure provides direct crystallographic evidence for Be12Fe precipitation in the 0.5Be alloy. As stated in the accompanying text, these precipitates typically distribute at austenite-ferrite boundaries in Chinese-character-like, rod-like or needle-like morphologies, occupying austenite matrix positions and consequently reducing austenite and ferrite contents with increasing beryllium addition.

Bright-field TEM micrographs and selected area diffraction pattern of as-cast Fe–30Mn–10Al–1.5C–1.5Be low-density steel. Panels (a) and (b) reveal different morphologies of Be12Fe precipitates, exhibiting large, irregular and intercrossing configurations distributed within the matrix. Panel (c) displays the morphology of the AlFe3 phase, while panel (d) provides the corresponding selected area diffraction pattern (SADP) confirming the presence of AlFe3 intermetallic compound; the diffraction spots are indexed to the {111}, {1\bar{1}1}, and {200} planes along the [011] zone axis. All micrographs in (a–c) share a 1 μm scale bar. The figure illustrates that 1.5 wt% Be addition induces significant precipitation of Be12Fe and AlFe3 phases in the as-cast microstructure, which encroach on the austenite matrix and contribute to reduced ferrite content and grain refinement compared with lower-Be alloys, thereby altering the microstructural evolution and mechanical response of the low-density steel.

This bar chart presents the Vickers microhardness (in HV) of as-cast Fe–30Mn–10Al–1.5C–xBe low-density steels as a function of beryllium content (x = 0, 0.5, 1.0, and 1.5). Red bars denote austenite hardness and blue bars denote ferrite hardness. In the 0Be and 0.5Be steels, only austenite is detected, exhibiting hardness values of 453.31 HV and 462.88 HV, respectively, with no ferrite phase present. In contrast, the 1.0Be and 1.5Be steels are dual-phase structures containing both austenite and ferrite; the austenite measures 475.80 HV and 479.74 HV, while the ferrite measures 437.40 HV and 452.54 HV, respectively. The data reveal a consistent upward trend in austenite hardness with increasing Be addition. The ferrite hardness similarly increases with Be content in the dual-phase alloys. The caption and text indicate that the matrix hardening upon Be addition is partly attributed to the formation of Be-containing compounds, and note that metallographic hardness of the Be–Fe phase in 0.5Be steel could not be obtained due to instrumental limitations. Overall, the figure demonstrates that Be alloying effectively enhances the hardness of both austenite and ferrite in these cast steels.

This figure presents DSC heating curves recorded between 1050 °C and 1350 °C for homogenised Fe–30Mn–10Al–1.5C–xBe low-density steels containing 0, 0.5, 1.0, and 1.5 wt% Be. The y-axis represents heat flow in mW/mg, and the x-axis indicates temperature. The 0Be alloy exhibits a single prominent thermal peak at 1320 °C, whereas the 0.5Be, 1.0Be, and 1.5Be alloys display progressively lower peak temperatures at 1165.9 °C, 1163.3 °C, and 1154.8 °C, respectively. The accompanying text reports that the Be-free steel transforms at 1278.8 °C and that Be addition reduces the transformation temperature by approximately 120 °C overall, with the most dramatic drop occurring when Be increases from 0 to 0.5 wt%, followed by modest further reductions of about 1 °C and 5 °C at 1.0 wt% and 1.5 wt%, respectively. These results indicate that beryllium strongly depresses the phase transition temperature, and its effect saturates with increasing content.

Figure 7 displays X-ray diffraction patterns of Fe–30Mn–10Al–1.5C–xBe (x = 0, 0.5, 1.0, 1.5) strips after rolling at 1050 °C, revealing the effect of Be addition on phase constitution. The baseline 0Be and 0.5Be alloys show only austenite (γ) diffraction peaks indexed as (111), (200), (220), (311), and (222), indicating a single-phase austenitic matrix under these conditions. At 1.0 wt% Be, new peaks assigned to δ-ferrite [(110), (200), (211)] appear alongside intermetallic reflections from Be11Fe [(112), (303)] and Be2Fe (212). Further increasing Be to 1.5 wt% intensifies the δ-ferrite and Be-Fe compound peaks while retaining the γ-austenite matrix. The progressive emergence of these phases confirms that Be solubility in austenite is limited; once saturated, excess Be segregates to form δ-ferrite surrounding austenite and nucleates Be11Fe and Be2Fe precipitates within the grains, supporting the proposed solidification and phase partitioning mechanism.

This figure comprises four optical microscopy (OM) micrographs illustrating the microstructure of Fe–30Mn–10Al–1.5C–xBe low-density steel strips after rolling at 1050 °C: (a) 0Be, (b) 0.5Be, (c) 1.0Be, and (d) 1.5Be. All images show a γ-Fe matrix with the rolling direction (RD) marked. The 0Be alloy exhibits equiaxed austenite grains containing sparse annealing twins. In the 0.5Be alloy, annealing twins are more prevalent, and discrete Be-Fe phase precipitates emerge along grain boundaries while the matrix remains austenitic. The 1.0Be microstructure features elongated grains aligned along RD, with increased Be-Fe phase and the appearance of α+Fe regions. In the 1.5Be alloy, grain elongation along RD is more pronounced, accompanied by substantial α-Fe and Be-Fe phases. Comparing the sequence, higher Be content correlates with increased precipitation and progressive morphological change from equiaxed to elongated austenite grains. These observations support that Be addition significantly influences phase constitution and as-rolled grain morphology in this steel system.

The figure presents SEM morphology and corresponding EDS elemental distribution maps of a Fe–30Mn–10Al–1.5C–1.5Be low-density steel strip after rolling at 1050 °C. Panel (a) shows the interfacial junction between austenite and ferrite, highlighting an elongated strip-shaped phase labeled A within a contrasting matrix that contains dispersed particles and pores. Panels (b)–(e) display the spatial distributions of Fe, Mn, C, and Al, respectively, each with a 1 μm scale bar. The Fe map appears relatively uniform across the field, whereas the Mn, C, and Al maps reveal localized contrasts between the elongated strip and the surrounding matrix. The caption reports that the inner strip-shaped structure is Mn-rich and Al-rich and the outer strip-shaped structure is Mn-rich and Al-poor, identifying the elongated phase as ferrite and the surrounding region as austenite. Overall, the figure supports the core conclusion that the rolled 1.5Be steel exhibits a dual-phase microstructure composed of elongated ferrite strips embedded in an austenitic matrix, with characteristic Mn and Al elemental partitioning between the two phases.

Figure 10 presents the tensile properties of Fe–30Mn–10Al–1.5C–xBe (x = 0, 0.5, 1.0, 1.5) low-density steels hot-rolled at 1050 °C. Subfigure (a) displays engineering stress–strain curves for the four compositions, accompanied by a dog-bone tensile specimen schematic with labeled dimensions, while subfigure (b) plots strain-hardening rate (dσ/dε) as a function of true strain. The 0.5Be alloy exhibits the highest elongation, with citation reporting approximately 40.47% total elongation, whereas ductility decreases when Be content exceeds 0.5%. Yield strength initially increases, then decreases, and finally rises with increasing Be, while tensile strength shows the opposite trend. The steady-state work-hardening rate is initially reduced by Be addition but gradually recovers as Be content increases. The 1.0Be and 1.5Be steels are dual-phase austenite (γ) plus ferrite (δ) microstructures, displaying lower plasticity than the fully austenitic 0Be and 0.5Be steels due to the weaker deformability of ferrite. These findings support that minor Be addition enhances ductility through solid solution strengthening and favorable Be–Fe phase formation, whereas higher Be levels promote ferrite and carbide precipitation, modifying strain-hardening behavior and reducing tensile strength.

Figure 11 presents a bar chart showing the matrix (austenite) microhardness of as-rolled Fe–30Mn–10Al–1.5C–xBe (x = 0, 0.5, 1.0, 1.5) low-density steels as a function of beryllium content, measured in HV. Four bars represent the Vickers hardness values of the austenite phase for steels with 0Be (415.55 HV), 0.5Be (470.97 HV), 1.0Be (398.39 HV), and 1.5Be (407.01 HV), respectively, each with error bars indicating measurement scatter. The hardness initially increases with Be addition, reaching a peak at 0.5Be, then drops significantly at 1.0Be before slightly recovering at 1.5Be. Notably, the 0.5Be alloy exhibits the highest austenite hardness (~470.97 HV), substantially exceeding the Be-free baseline (~415.55 HV). While the chart explicitly labels only austenite hardness, the caption notes that the tested material is the rolling structure, and accompanying text also reports ferrite hardness values of 349.81 HV and 371.50 HV for the 1.0Be and 1.5Be steels, respectively. The trend demonstrates that moderate Be addition (0.5%) effectively hardens the austenite matrix, whereas higher Be levels (≥1.0%) reduce the hardness, suggesting an optimal Be content around 0.5% for maximizing matrix strength in this low-density steel system.

Figure 12 presents SEM micrographs of tensile fracture surfaces for Fe–30Mn–10Al–1.5C–xBe low-density steels, comparing (a) 0Be, (b) 0.5Be, (c) 1.0Be, and (d) 1.5Be alloys. Each subfigure contains an upper-right inset showing the macroscopic fracture profile with a 1 mm scale bar, where a white square marks the microscale region magnified in the main image. The 0Be steel (a) exhibits ductile fracture morphology with dimples; the caption reports that spherical inclusions reside within these dimples in the fibrous zone (Area A1), and that dissociation planes and river patterns occur in the macroscopic Area A2. As beryllium content increases from 0.5Be (b) to 1.0Be (c) and 1.5Be (d), the fracture mode progressively transitions toward brittle cleavage, evidenced by increasing cleavage facets and the appearance of cleavage platforms in (c) and annotated cleavage steps in (d). This systematic evolution from ductile dimple rupture to cleavage-dominated fracture demonstrates that rising Be content fundamentally alters the tensile failure mechanism in these austenitic steels.

Figure 13 presents electron backscatter diffraction (EBSD) characterization of Fe–30Mn–10Al–1.5C–xBe low-density steels rolled at 1050 °C. The figure is organized in four rows (a–d) corresponding to 0Be, 0.5Be, 1.0Be, and 1.5Be alloys, each containing four columns: IQ maps showing grain boundary networks with colored lines, IPF maps revealing crystallographic orientations, KAM maps indicating local misorientation distributions, and phase maps distinguishing austenite (green) from ferrite (red). As the Be content increases from row (a) to (d), the phase maps show a progressive increase in ferrite fraction within the austenite matrix. The KAM maps display localized stress concentration, with caption noting these correspond to carbide and Be–Fe precipitates concentrated at grain boundaries and phase interfaces. The IPF color key indicates orientations relative to the rolling direction (RD).

Figure 14 presents grain size distribution histograms of Fe–30Mn–10Al–1.5C–xBe low-density steels obtained from EBSD analysis, where subplots (a), (b), (c), and (d) correspond to steels containing 0, 0.5, 1.0, and 1.5 wt% Be, respectively. The horizontal axis represents grain diameter in micrometers and the vertical axis indicates area fraction in percent. The 0Be steel shows a unimodal distribution centered near 15 μm. Introducing 0.5Be causes pronounced grain coarsening, with the peak shifting to approximately 40 μm and a wider spread from 5 to 45 μm. Conversely, the 1.0Be steel exhibits marked grain refinement, featuring a sharp peak around 10 μm and a narrow distribution between 4 and 20 μm. The 1.5Be steel reveals a broader, non-uniform distribution with a dominant peak near 12 μm and a distinct secondary population around 25 μm, suggesting heterogeneous grain development. These statistics confirm that Be content critically governs grain size evolution in this lightweight steel system.

Fig. 15 presents bright-field TEM images and selected area electron diffraction (SAED) patterns characterizing the microstructures of rolled Fe–30Mn–10Al–1.5C–xBe low-density steels with Be contents x = 0, 0.5, 1.0, and 1.5. Panels (a1)–(d1) display the austenitic matrix morphologies of 0Be, 0.5Be, 1.0Be, and 1.5Be steels, respectively, with (d1) additionally highlighting grain boundaries (GB) and dislocations marked by red arrows. Panels (a2), (b2), and (d2) show SAED spot patterns from the γ-Fe austenite matrix along [1̄14], [1̄11], and [013] zone axes, respectively. Panels (a3)–(d3) provide higher-magnification views of carbide particles in each alloy. Corresponding carbide SAED patterns in (a4)–(d4) identify the 0Be steel carbide as κ-carbide (zone axis [001]) and the carbides in 0.5Be, 1.0Be, and 1.5Be steels as Be2C (zone axes [1̄12], [011], and [1̄12], respectively). These results confirm that Be addition transforms the precipitate phase from κ-carbide to Be2C, establishing the crystallographic basis for phase evolution in these steels.

Figure 16 presents bright-field TEM images showing dislocation structures in hot-rolled Fe–30Mn–10Al–1.5C–xBe low-density steels with varying beryllium contents. Subfigures (a), (b) and (c) correspond to steels containing 0.5Be, 1.0Be and 1.5Be, respectively, all captured at a scale bar of 2 μm. White arrows in each micrograph highlight dislocation features. The images reveal a progressive increase in dislocation density as Be content rises from 0.5% to 1.5%. The 0.5Be steel exhibits relatively sparse dislocations, while the 1.0Be steel shows increased dislocation lines and tangles. In the 1.5Be steel, the microstructure becomes considerably denser with highly tangled dislocations concentrated near grain boundaries or sub-boundaries. This visual trend indicates that Be addition promotes dislocation multiplication during rolling. The caption and citation confirm that these TEM observations are consistent with EBSD kernel average misorientation (KAM) statistics, supporting the conclusion that increasing Be content enhances dislocation density and thereby strengthens the steel.

This figure presents a schematic diagram illustrating the microstructural evolution of grains and precipitates in rolling-state Fe–30Mn–10Al–1.5C–xBe low-density steels as beryllium content increases from x = 0 to 1.5. Panel (a) for the 0Be alloy depicts a single γ-austenite matrix containing annealing twins and no visible precipitates. Panel (b) for 0.5Be shows the initial appearance of small orange precipitated phases primarily located at grain boundaries and within γ grains. Panel (c) for 1.0Be exhibits a higher number density of these precipitates alongside the emergence of coarse green α-phase inclusions. Panel (d) for 1.5Be demonstrates a further increased volume fraction of both the precipitated phases and α-phase particles dispersed throughout the γ matrix. Collectively, the sequence indicates that progressive Be addition promotes the nucleation and growth of secondary precipitates and the α-phase, thereby significantly altering the phase constitution and microstructural heterogeneity of the rolled steel.

This figure comprises three schematic crystal structure models illustrating the solid solution behavior of Be, Al, and C in the Fe matrix of Fe–30Mn–10Al–1.5C–xBe low-density steels. The first model depicts a substitutional Be atom (blue) occupying a regular Fe lattice site (green), and caption states that surrounding Fe atoms move closer to the smaller Be solute, causing inward lattice shrinkage. The second model shows a substitutional Al atom (orange) in the Fe lattice; its visibly larger atomic radius compared to Fe crowds out neighboring Fe atoms and produces outward lattice expansion. The third model presents a close-packed Fe atomic plane with small purple C atoms occupying interstitial sites. Collectively, the schematics contrast opposite distortion directions: Al increases molar volume and effectively reduces steel density, whereas Be-induced inward expansion limits lightweighting. The figure supports the core conclusion that distinct lattice distortions raise solid solution energy and instability, and that excessive Be beyond the austenite solubility limit promotes Be–Fe phase precipitation and expels interstitial C to form carbide precipitates.

The figure displays the binary Fe–Be equilibrium phase diagram, with temperature (°C) on the vertical axis and Fe/(Be+Fe) molar ratio (0–1) on the horizontal axis. Key phase fields identified include Liquid, BCC-A2 (ferrite), FCC-A1 (austenite), HCP-A3, and intermetallic line compounds FeBe₁₂, FeBe₅, and FeBe₂, together with associated invariant reactions. The diagram indicates that BCC-A2 accommodates markedly higher Be solubility than FCC-A1, whereas the austenite phase exhibits very limited Be dissolution. As stated in the caption, this map describes the thermodynamic equilibrium relationship between Be and Fe components. Supporting text notes that Be solubility in austenite is less than 1%, forcing excess Be to precipitate as intermetallic compounds or partition to ferrite. This phase-diagram evidence explains the microstructural trend in Fe–30Mn–10Al–1.5C–xBe low-density steels: increasing Be beyond solid-solubility limits leads to ferrite formation around austenite grains and Be-rich compound precipitation within the austenite matrix.

Figure 20 is a scatter plot comparing the product of strength and ductility (in GPa·%) versus density (g/cm³) for Be low-density steels against various other metallic materials. The Be steels, labeled Fe-30Mn-10Al-1.5C-(0.5-1.5)Be and marked as 0.5Be (square), 1.0Be (circle), and 1.5Be (triangle), cluster within a red ellipse in the low-density region near 6.35–6.55 g/cm³, exhibiting strength-ductility products ranging from approximately 22 to 46 GPa·%. These values exceed or rival those of many conventional steels—such as 40Cr, GCr15, Q235, and stainless steels like 0Cr13 and 1Cr23Ni18—which occupy higher densities between 7.6 and 7.9 g/cm³. Other lightweight steels, including Fe-20Mn-9Al-1.2C-3Cr and Fe-Mn-Al-C variants, plot at intermediate densities. A dashed curve traces an apparent upper-performance envelope. The caption states that Be steel density is 21.38% lower than pure iron while preserving a high strength-ductility product. The figure supports the conclusion that the developed Be steels define a new, favorable region in the density-strength-ductility property space, combining substantial lightweighting with excellent comprehensive mechanical performance.
